hints: Each connected component is a group of nodes reachable from each other.
Union-Find: start with n components, each union reduces the count by 1.
Alternatively, count the number of DFS/BFS traversals needed to visit all nodes.
approach: Use Union-Find starting with n components. For each edge, union the two nodes — if they were in different components, decrement the component count. Return the final count.
time_complexity_explanation: Each edge triggers a union operation, which is nearly O(1) amortized with path compression. Processing all edges is O(n + e * α(n)).
space_complexity_explanation: The Union-Find parent and rank arrays store n entries.
key_insight: Start with n components; each edge that successfully unions two different components reduces the count by exactly one.

title: Graph Valid Tree
hints: A valid tree has exactly n-1 edges and is fully connected (no cycles).
If edges != n-1, it's immediately not a tree.
Use Union-Find to check for cycles: if an edge connects already-connected nodes, there's a cycle.
approach: Check if edges == n-1. Then use Union-Find to verify no cycles: process each edge, and if both endpoints already share a root, a cycle exists. If no cycle and edge count is correct, it's a valid tree.
time_complexity_explanation: We check the edge count in O(1), then process each edge with a near-O(1) union-find operation.
space_complexity_explanation: The Union-Find parent and rank arrays store n entries.
key_insight: A graph is a valid tree iff it has exactly n-1 edges and no cycles — Union-Find checks both properties efficiently.

title: Word Ladder
hints: Each word is a node; edges connect words that differ by exactly one letter.
BFS finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
Use wildcard patterns (e.g., h*t for hot) to efficiently find neighbors.
approach: BFS from beginWord. Build an adjacency list using wildcard patterns: for each word, replace each character with '*' to create patterns. Words sharing a pattern are neighbors. BFS level by level to find the shortest transformation sequence.
time_complexity: O(m^2 * n) where m=word length, n=word count
time_complexity_explanation: Building the adjacency list with wildcard patterns takes O(m² * n) — for each of n words, m patterns of length m. BFS visits each word once.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list with wildcard pattern keys stores O(m² * n) entries.
key_insight: Wildcard patterns (h*t) create an implicit graph where words differing by one letter share a pattern — BFS on this graph finds the shortest transformation.

title: Climbing Stairs
hints: The number of ways to reach step n depends on the previous steps.
You can reach step n from step n-1 (one step) or step n-2 (two steps).
This is exactly the Fibonacci sequence: dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2].
approach: Bottom-up DP. The number of ways to reach step i is dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Base cases: dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2. Only need two variables since each state depends on just the previous two.
time_complexity_explanation: A single loop from step 3 to n, computing each value in O(1) from the previous two.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables are needed to track the previous two step counts.
key_insight: This is the Fibonacci sequence in disguise — each step's count is the sum of the two preceding steps because you can arrive via a 1-step or 2-step jump.

title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
hints: At each step, you choose to come from one step back or two steps back.
dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]).
You can start from step 0 or step 1, so the answer is min of the last two dp values.
approach: Bottom-up DP. For each step i, the minimum cost to reach it is cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). The answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) since you can reach the top from either of the last two steps.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the n steps, computing each minimum cost in O(1) from the previous two values.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables track the previous two minimum costs, no array needed.
key_insight: Each step accumulates the minimum cost to reach it; the answer considers both possible final jumps (from last or second-to-last step).

title: House Robber
hints: At each house, you either rob it (add its value to the best from two houses back) or skip it.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).
You only need two variables to track the previous two optimal values.
approach: Bottom-up DP with two variables. For each house, compute max(skip this house = prev max, rob this house = two-back max + current value). Update the two tracking variables.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the n houses, making an O(1) max decision at each house.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables track the maximum profit at the previous two houses.
key_insight: The optimal robbery at each house is the better of skipping it (keep previous max) or robbing it (current value + max from two houses back, avoiding adjacency).

title: House Robber II
hints: Houses are arranged in a circle — the first and last are adjacent.
You can't rob both the first and last house.
Run House Robber twice: once excluding the last house, once excluding the first.
approach: The circular constraint means you can't rob both house 0 and house n-1. Run the linear House Robber on nums[:-1] and nums[1:], return the max of both results.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes of House Robber (one on nums[0..n-2], one on nums[1..n-1]), each O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Each linear pass uses only two tracking variables, same as House Robber.
key_insight: Break the circular dependency by running the linear solution twice — once excluding first house, once excluding last — and taking the maximum.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
hints: A palindrome mirrors around its center.
Try expanding outward from each possible center (both odd and even length).
There are 2n-1 possible centers (n single chars + n-1 gaps between chars).
approach: Expand around center. For each index, try both odd-length (single center) and even-length (two-char center) palindromes. Expand outward while characters match. Track the longest found.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n centers, expansion takes O(n) in the worst case, giving O(n²) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Only variables for the current center, expansion bounds, and the best result are needed.
key_insight: Every palindrome has a center — expanding outward from each of the 2n-1 possible centers finds all palindromic substrings in O(n^2) time.

title: Palindromic Substrings
hints: Similar to longest palindromic substring, but count all palindromes.
Expand around each center and count each valid expansion as a palindrome.
Remember to check both odd and even length palindromes.
approach: Expand around center for each position. For both odd and even centers, expand while characters match and increment the count for each valid palindrome found.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n possible centers (both odd and even), expanding outward takes O(n) worst case, giving O(n²).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a counter and expansion variables are needed, no extra data structures.
key_insight: Each successful expansion from a center produces one more palindromic substring — count every expansion step rather than just tracking the longest.

title: Decode Ways
hints: At each position, you can decode one digit or two digits (if valid).
dp[i] = dp[i-1] (if single digit valid) + dp[i-2] (if two digits valid).
'0' can only be decoded as part of '10' or '20' — it can't stand alone.
approach: Bottom-up DP. At each position, if the current digit is valid (1-9), add dp[i-1]. If the two-digit number ending here is valid (10-26), add dp[i-2]. Handle '0' carefully — it can only pair with '1' or '2'.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the string of length n, computing each dp value in O(1) from at most two previous values.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array stores one value per character position in the string.
key_insight: Each position branches into 1-digit or 2-digit decodings (like Fibonacci), but '0' kills the single-digit branch and two-digit values must be 10-26.

title: Coin Change
hints: For each amount, try every coin denomination.
dp[amount] = min(dp[amount - coin] + 1) for each coin.
Initialize dp[0]=0 and all others to infinity (amount+1 works as sentinel).
approach: Bottom-up DP. For each amount from 1 to target, try each coin: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin] + 1). Initialize dp[0]=0. If dp[amount] exceeds amount, return -1 (impossible).
time_complexity_explanation: Two nested loops: the outer iterates over amounts 1 to target, and the inner tries each coin, giving O(amount × coins).
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array stores one value for each amount from 0 to the target.
key_insight: Each amount builds on smaller subproblems — using coin c means 1 + the optimal solution for (amount - c).

title: Maximum Product Subarray
hints: Unlike maximum sum, a negative number can flip min to max (and vice versa).
Track both the current maximum AND minimum product at each position.
When encountering a negative number, swap max and min before multiplying.
approach: Track curMax and curMin at each position. For each number, compute new max and min considering: the number itself, number*curMax, number*curMin. Update the global maximum.
solution_python:
def maxProduct(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
    res = max(nums)
    curMin, curMax = 1, 1
    for n in nums:
        vals = (n, n * curMax, n * curMin)
        curMax, curMin = max(vals), min(vals)
        res = max(res, curMax)
    return res
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array, updating curMax and curMin in O(1) at each element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three variables are needed: curMax, curMin, and the global maximum.
key_insight: Negative numbers can flip min to max — tracking both the running maximum and minimum product handles sign changes correctly.

title: Word Break
hints: dp[i] represents whether s[0:i] can be segmented into dictionary words.
For each position i, check all possible last words ending at i.
dp[i] = true if there exists j where dp[j] is true and s[j:i] is in the dictionary.
approach: Bottom-up DP. dp[i] is true if s[0:i] can be segmented. For each position i, check if any word in the dictionary matches s[j:i] where dp[j] is true. dp[0] = true (empty string).
time_complexity: O(n * m * k) where m=dict size, k=avg word length
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n positions, we check each of the m dictionary words, and each comparison can take up to k characters.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array stores one boolean per position in the string.
key_insight: Each position is segmentable if it matches a dictionary word AND the remaining suffix is also segmentable — classic DP with string matching.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
hints: dp[i] = length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i.
For each i, check all j < i: if nums[j] < nums[i], dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1).
For O(n log n), maintain a tails array and use binary search.
approach: DP approach: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]. Each element starts with dp[i]=1. Return the maximum dp value. O(n^2) time.
time_complexity_explanation: Two nested loops: for each of the n elements, we check all previous elements, giving O(n²) comparisons.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array stores the longest increasing subsequence length ending at each of the n elements.
key_insight: Each element extends the best subsequence ending at any smaller previous element — dp[i] considers all valid predecessors.

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
hints: If the total sum is odd, partitioning into equal halves is impossible.
The problem reduces to: can we find a subset that sums to total/2?
Use a DP set tracking all achievable sums; for each number, add it to existing sums.
approach: If total sum is odd, return false. Otherwise, find if a subset sums to total/2. Use a set of achievable sums. For each number, create new sums by adding it to each existing sum in the set.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n numbers, we iterate over the current achievable sums (up to target/2), giving O(n × sum).
space_complexity_explanation: The set of achievable sums can contain up to O(sum) distinct values.
key_insight: Equal partition = finding a subset summing to total/2. This is the 0/1 knapsack problem, solvable with a DP set of achievable sums.

title: Unique Paths
hints: You can only move right or down — each cell is reachable from the cell above or to the left.
The first row and first column all have exactly 1 path (straight line).
approach: Fill a 2D DP grid where dp[r][c] = number of paths to reach (r,c). First row and column are all 1s. Each other cell sums paths from above and left.
time_complexity_explanation: We fill each of the m × n cells once, with O(1) work per cell.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single row of n values is needed at a time (optimized from the full m × n grid).
key_insight: Only right and down moves means each cell's path count is the sum from above and left — optimizable to a single row of DP.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
hints: Compare characters at each position in both strings.
If characters match, LCS extends by 1 from the diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1]+1).
If they don't match, take the max of skipping one character from either string.
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = LCS length of text1[0:i] and text2[0:j]. If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
time_complexity_explanation: We fill each of the m × n cells in the DP table once, with O(1) work per cell.
space_complexity_explanation: The full m × n DP table is stored to compute the LCS length.
key_insight: Character match extends the diagonal LCS by 1; mismatch takes the best of either skip — this naturally builds the optimal subsequence length.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
hints: At each day, you're in one of two states: holding a stock or not holding.
After selling, you must wait one day (cooldown) before buying again.
Model as a state machine with states: buy, sell, cooldown.
approach: Use DP with states. At each day: if buying, profit = max(keep holding, buy today from cooldown state). If selling, profit = max(stay idle, sell today). Track cooldown by using the profit from 2 days ago for buying.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the n days, updating O(1) state variables (buy, sell, cooldown) at each day.
space_complexity_explanation: Three state arrays (or variables with optimization) track the profit for each day's possible actions.
key_insight: The cooldown constraint means after selling at day i, the next buy can't happen until day i+2 — model this with a state machine DP.

title: Coin Change II
hints: Count the number of combinations (not permutations) that sum to the amount.
Process coins one at a time to avoid counting different orderings of the same coins.
dp[a] += dp[a - coin] for each coin, processing coins in the outer loop.
approach: Unbounded knapsack DP. Process each coin type in the outer loop (to avoid duplicate orderings). For each amount, dp[a] += dp[a - coin]. dp[0] = 1 (one way to make amount 0).
time_complexity_explanation: The outer loop processes each coin type and the inner loop iterates over all amounts, giving O(amount × coins).
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array stores the number of combinations for each amount from 0 to the target.
key_insight: Processing coins in the outer loop ensures each combination is counted once (not permutations) — coin ordering is fixed.

title: Target Sum
hints: Each number is either added or subtracted — think of it as partitioning into two groups.
Use DP where the state is (index, current sum).
Alternatively, this reduces to a subset sum problem: find subsets summing to (total+target)/2.
approach: Use memoized DFS with state (index, current_sum). At each index, branch into adding or subtracting the current number. Count paths that reach the target at the end.
time_complexity_explanation: The DFS branches twice at each of the n elements (add or subtract), but memoization bounds unique states to O(n × sum).
space_complexity_explanation: The memoization cache stores results for up to O(n × sum) unique (index, current_sum) states.
key_insight: Each number creates a binary branch (+/-), but many paths reach the same sum — DP collapses identical states to avoid exponential computation.

title: Interleaving String
hints: At each position in s3, the character must come from either s1 or s2 (in order).
Use 2D DP where dp[i][j] = can s1[0:i] and s2[0:j] interleave to form s3[0:i+j]?
Check if the current s3 character matches the next unused character from s1 or s2.
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] indicates whether s1[0:i] and s2[0:j] can form s3[0:i+j]. Transition: if s1[i-1]==s3[i+j-1], check dp[i-1][j]; if s2[j-1]==s3[i+j-1], check dp[i][j-1].
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m × n cells in the DP table is computed once in O(1), giving O(m × n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: The full m × n DP table stores boolean values for each (i, j) subproblem.
key_insight: Two pointers (i into s1, j into s2) define the state — at position i+j in s3, we can take from s1[i] or s2[j] if they match.

title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
hints: From each cell, DFS to all four neighbors with strictly greater values.
The increasing constraint means there are no cycles — perfect for memoization.
dp[r][c] = 1 + max(dp of all valid increasing neighbors).
approach: DFS with memoization from every cell. For each cell, explore all four neighbors with strictly greater values. Cache results since the strictly increasing constraint guarantees no cycles.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is computed once via DFS with memoization, and each computation does O(1) work per neighbor.
space_complexity_explanation: The memoization table stores one value per cell, using O(m × n) space.
key_insight: Strictly increasing values guarantee a DAG (no cycles), making memoization safe — each cell's result is computed exactly once.

title: Distinct Subsequences
hints: dp[i][j] = number of ways to form t[0:j] from s[0:i].
If s[i] == t[j], we can either use this match or skip s[i].
If s[i] != t[j], we must skip s[i]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = number of distinct subsequences of s[0:i] that equal t[0:j]. If s[i]==t[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j]. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m × n cells in the DP table is computed once with O(1) work.
space_complexity_explanation: The full m × n DP table is used to store subsequence counts for each subproblem.
key_insight: When characters match, you have two choices (use the match + skip it), combining both counts. When they don't match, you must skip the s character.

title: Edit Distance
hints: dp[i][j] = min operations to convert word1[0:i] to word2[0:j].
If characters match, no operation needed: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1].
If they differ, try insert (dp[i][j-1]+1), delete (dp[i-1][j]+1), or replace (dp[i-1][j-1]+1).
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = min edits to convert word1[0:i] to word2[0:j]. If chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise, 1 + min(insert, delete, replace) from adjacent states.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m × n cells in the DP table is filled once, considering three possible operations in O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The m × n DP table stores the edit distance for each pair of prefixes.
key_insight: Three operations map to three adjacent DP cells: insert=right, delete=down, replace=diagonal — take the minimum plus one operation cost.

title: Burst Balloons
hints: Think about which balloon to burst LAST in a range, not first.
If balloon k is burst last in range [i,j], its value is nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1].
dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons between i and j.
approach: Interval DP. For range [l,r], try each balloon i as the LAST to burst. Its coins = nums[l-1]*nums[i]*nums[r+1] (boundaries remain). dp[l][r] = max over all i of (dp[l][i-1] + coins + dp[i+1][r]).
time_complexity_explanation: Three nested loops: two for the interval endpoints (l, r) and one for the last balloon choice within the interval, giving O(n³).
space_complexity_explanation: The 2D DP table stores the maximum coins for each of the O(n²) intervals.
key_insight: Thinking about which balloon to burst LAST (not first) in a range makes the subproblems independent — the boundaries are fixed.

title: Regular Expression Matching
hints: '.' matches any single character. '*' means zero or more of the preceding element.
dp[i][j] = does s[0:i] match p[0:j]?
For '*', consider two cases: use it zero times (skip pattern pair) or use it once more (if current char matches).
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = whether s[0:i] matches p[0:j]. For '*': either skip the pattern pair (zero matches: dp[i][j-2]) or match one more character if current matches. For '.': matches any character.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m × n cells in the DP table is computed once, with O(1) transitions per cell.
space_complexity_explanation: The m × n DP table stores match results for each (string position, pattern position) pair.
key_insight: The '*' operator has two DP transitions: skip the pattern pair entirely (zero matches) or consume one more character from s (if it matches the preceding pattern element).

title: Maximum Subarray
hints: At each position, decide: extend the current subarray or start a new one?
If the running sum becomes negative, it's better to start fresh.
Track the current sum and the maximum sum seen so far.
approach: Kadane's algorithm. Iterate through the array, maintaining a running sum. If the running sum goes negative, reset to 0. Track the maximum subarray sum encountered.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the n elements, updating the running sum and maximum in O(1) per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables (current sum and maximum sum) are needed.
key_insight: A negative running sum only hurts future subarrays — reset to zero and start fresh, since any extension would be worse than starting over.

title: Jump Game
hints: Track the farthest index you can reach as you iterate.
If you ever reach an index beyond your current farthest, you're stuck.
If the farthest reach includes the last index, return true.
approach: Greedy: track the farthest reachable index. Iterate through the array — at each position, update the farthest reach. If the current index exceeds the farthest, return false. If farthest >= last index, return true.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array, updating the farthest reachable index in O(1) at each position.
space_complexity_explanation: Only one variable tracks the farthest reachable index.
key_insight: Work backwards: if a position can reach the current goal, it becomes the new goal. If the goal reaches index 0, the end is reachable.

title: Jump Game II
hints: Think of this as BFS where each 'level' is one jump.
Track the current jump's farthest reach and the overall farthest.
When you reach the end of the current level, increment jumps and start the next level.
approach: BFS-like greedy. Track the end of the current jump range and the farthest reachable. When you reach the current range end, take a jump (increment count) and extend to the farthest reachable.
solution_python:
def jump(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
    jumps = 0
    l = r = 0
    while r < len(nums) - 1:
        farthest = 0
        for i in range(l, r + 1):
            farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
        l = r + 1
        r = farthest
        jumps += 1
    return jumps
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array, updating the farthest reach and jump count in O(1) per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few tracking variables (current end, farthest, jump count) are needed.
key_insight: Each jump explores a 'level' of reachable indices (like BFS) — the minimum jumps equals the number of BFS levels to reach the end.

title: Gas Station
hints: If total gas >= total cost, a solution must exist.
If you run out of gas at station j starting from i, no station between i and j works either.
Reset the starting point to j+1 whenever the tank goes negative.
approach: If total gas < total cost, return -1. Otherwise, greedily find the start: iterate, tracking the tank. If tank goes negative, reset start to the next station and reset tank. The final start position is the answer.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through all n stations, computing cumulative gas surplus/deficit in O(1) per station.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables track the total surplus, current tank, and starting station.
key_insight: If you fail at station j from start i, all stations between i and j also fail — so jump the start to j+1, combining multiple failure proofs in one pass.

title: Hand of Straights
hints: Each group must be W consecutive cards — start each group from the smallest available card.
Sort the cards and greedily form groups starting from the smallest unused card.
Use a frequency map to track remaining cards.
approach: Sort the cards. For each smallest available card, try to form a group of W consecutive cards by decrementing their counts. If any card in the sequence is unavailable, return false.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n), then forming groups requires scanning through all n cards.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency counter stores up to n distinct card values.
key_insight: Greedily start each group from the smallest available card — this forces a unique grouping and quickly reveals if valid groups are impossible.

title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
hints: A triplet is usable only if none of its values exceed the corresponding target values.
Filter out triplets where any value exceeds the target.
From remaining triplets, check if each target value appears in at least one triplet.
approach: Filter triplets: discard any where a[i] > target[i] for any position (merging would exceed target). From remaining valid triplets, check if the max in each position can reach each target value.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through all n triplets, checking each against the target in O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: Only three boolean flags track whether each target position has been matched.
key_insight: Triplets with any value exceeding the target are unusable (merge only takes max). Among valid triplets, we just need each target position covered by at least one.

title: Partition Labels
hints: Each character must appear in exactly one partition.
For each character, find its last occurrence — the partition must extend at least that far.
Expand the partition end as you encounter characters with later last occurrences.
approach: Record the last occurrence of each character. Iterate through the string, expanding the current partition's end to include the last occurrence of each character. When the current index equals the partition end, close the partition.
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes through the string: one to record last occurrences, one to determine partitions.
space_complexity_explanation: The last-occurrence map stores at most 26 entries (one per lowercase letter).
key_insight: A partition must contain all occurrences of each character in it — tracking last occurrences and expanding greedily finds minimum partitions.

title: Valid Parenthesis String
hints: '*' can be '(', ')' or empty — track a range of possible open counts.
Maintain leftMin (min possible open parens) and leftMax (max possible open parens).
If leftMax goes negative, too many ')'. If leftMin goes negative, clamp to 0.
approach: Track the range of possible open parenthesis counts [leftMin, leftMax]. '(' increments both, ')' decrements both, '*' decrements leftMin and increments leftMax. Clamp leftMin to 0. Valid if leftMin reaches 0.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the string, updating the possible open-count range in O(1) per character.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables (leftMin and leftMax) track the range of possible open counts.
key_insight: Instead of trying all '*' possibilities, track the range [min, max] of possible open counts — if 0 is within range at the end, some valid assignment exists.

title: Insert Interval
hints: Split intervals into three groups: before, overlapping with, and after the new interval.
Non-overlapping intervals before/after are added directly.
Merge overlapping intervals by taking min of starts and max of ends.
approach: Iterate through intervals. Add all intervals that end before newInterval starts. Merge all overlapping intervals with newInterval (update start/end). Add all remaining intervals after the merged interval.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the sorted intervals, with O(1) comparison and merge operations per interval.
space_complexity_explanation: The result list stores up to n intervals in the worst case (no overlaps).
key_insight: Three cases per interval: entirely before (add as-is), overlapping (merge), entirely after (add remaining). The merged interval grows to encompass all overlaps.

title: Merge Intervals
hints: Sort intervals by start time so overlapping intervals are adjacent.
If the current interval overlaps with the last merged interval, extend the end.
Two intervals overlap if the current start <= the previous end.
approach: Sort intervals by start time. Initialize result with the first interval. For each subsequent interval, if it overlaps with the last result interval (start <= last end), merge by extending end. Otherwise, add as new interval.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n), and the subsequent merge pass is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The result list stores up to n merged intervals.
key_insight: Sorting by start time ensures overlapping intervals are consecutive — a single pass then merges them by extending end times.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
hints: Sort by end time — greedily keep intervals that end earliest.
When two intervals overlap, remove the one with the later end time.
Counting removals = total intervals minus the maximum non-overlapping set.
approach: Sort by end time. Greedily keep the interval with the earliest end time when overlaps occur. Count the number of intervals that need to be removed.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting by end time takes O(n log n), and the greedy scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is in-place, and only a counter and a tracking variable are needed.
key_insight: Sort by end time and greedily keep earliest-ending intervals — this maximizes non-overlapping intervals, minimizing removals.

title: Meeting Rooms
hints: A person can attend all meetings only if no two meetings overlap.
Sort by start time and check if any meeting starts before the previous one ends.
If intervals[i].start < intervals[i-1].end, there's a conflict.
approach: Sort intervals by start time. Check each consecutive pair: if the current meeting starts before the previous one ends, return false (overlap detected). If no overlaps found, return true.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the intervals takes O(n log n), and the overlap check is a single O(n) pass.
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in-place with no additional data structures needed.
key_insight: Sorting by start time makes overlap detection trivial — just check if each meeting starts before the previous one ends.

title: Meeting Rooms II
hints: The minimum rooms needed = the maximum number of overlapping meetings at any point.
Use a sweep line: +1 at each start time, -1 at each end time.
The maximum running count of active meetings is the answer.
approach: Separate start and end times. Sort both arrays. Use two pointers: when a start < current end, a new room is needed (increment count). When end <= start, a room is freed. Track the maximum count.
solution_python:
def minMeetingRooms(self, intervals: List[List[int]]) -> int:
    starts = sorted(i[0] for i in intervals)
    ends = sorted(i[1] for i in intervals)
    res = count = 0
    s = e = 0
    while s < len(starts):
        if starts[s] < ends[e]:
            count += 1
            s += 1
        else:
            count -= 1
            e += 1
        res = max(res, count)
    return res
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the start and end time arrays each takes O(n log n), and the two-pointer scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Two separate arrays of size n store the start and end times.
key_insight: Separating and sorting start/end times creates a sweep line — tracking concurrent meetings with two pointers finds the peak overlap.

title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
hints: Sort both intervals and queries. Process queries in order of increasing value.
Use a min-heap keyed by interval size to track candidate intervals.
For each query, add all intervals that start <= query, then remove expired ones (end < query).
approach: Sort intervals by start and queries by value. For each query (in order), push all intervals starting <= query onto a min-heap (keyed by size). Pop intervals that end before the query. The heap top is the smallest valid interval.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting intervals and queries takes O((n + q) log n). Each interval is pushed/popped from the heap once.
space_complexity_explanation: The heap and sorted query arrays together use O(n + q) space.
key_insight: Processing sorted queries with a min-heap of interval sizes — lazily adding and removing intervals — efficiently finds the smallest containing interval for each query.

title: Rotate Image
hints: Rotating 90° clockwise = transpose + reverse each row.
Transpose swaps matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i].
After transposing, reversing each row completes the rotation.
approach: In-place rotation: first transpose the matrix (swap across the main diagonal), then reverse each row. This achieves a 90° clockwise rotation.
time_complexity_explanation: The transpose visits each of the n² cells once, and reversing each row is O(n), giving O(n²) total.
space_complexity_explanation: All operations are done in-place by swapping elements, no extra matrix needed.
key_insight: 90° clockwise rotation = transpose + reverse rows. This decomposition makes in-place rotation simple with just swaps.

title: Spiral Matrix
hints: Traverse the matrix in layers: top row → right column → bottom row → left column.
Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right.
Shrink boundaries after each direction and stop when they cross.
approach: Use four boundaries (top, bottom, left, right). Traverse right along top row, down right column, left along bottom row, up left column. Shrink boundaries after each direction. Stop when boundaries cross.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m × n cells is visited exactly once during the spiral traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: Only four boundary variables are used (excluding the output list).
key_insight: Four shrinking boundaries (top/bottom/left/right) define the unvisited rectangle — traverse each edge in order and shrink inward.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
hints: Use the first row and first column as markers for which rows/columns should be zeroed.
First pass: mark the first cell of each row/column that contains a zero.
Second pass: zero out cells based on markers, then handle the first row/column separately.
approach: Use the first row and column as flags. Track if the first row/column themselves need zeroing. First pass marks zeros in first row/column. Second pass zeros cells based on markers. Finally handle first row/column.
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes through the m × n matrix: one to mark zeros and one to apply them.
space_complexity_explanation: The first row and column serve as in-place markers, requiring only two extra boolean flags.
key_insight: Reuse the first row and column as markers to achieve O(1) space — just need an extra flag for the first row since it overlaps with column markers.

title: Happy Number
hints: Repeatedly replace the number by the sum of squares of its digits.
If you reach 1, it's happy. If you see a repeated number, it's a cycle (not happy).
Use a set to detect cycles, or use Floyd's cycle detection (fast/slow pointers).
approach: Compute sum of squared digits repeatedly. Use a set to track seen numbers. If we reach 1, return true. If we see a repeated number (cycle), return false.
time_complexity: O(log n) per step, bounded iterations
time_complexity_explanation: Each step computes the sum of squared digits in O(log n) time (number of digits). The process is bounded by the cycle detection.
space_complexity_explanation: The set stores previously seen numbers, which is bounded by the cycle length (at most O(log n) distinct values).
key_insight: The digit-square-sum sequence either reaches 1 or enters a cycle — detecting the cycle (via set or Floyd's algorithm) determines happiness.

title: Plus One
hints: Start from the last digit and add 1.
If the digit becomes 10, set it to 0 and carry 1 to the next digit.
If the carry propagates past the first digit (e.g., 999 + 1), prepend a 1.
approach: Iterate from the last digit. If digit < 9, increment and return. If digit is 9, set to 0 and continue (carry). If all digits were 9, prepend 1 (e.g., 999 → 1000).
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass from the last digit to the first, with O(1) work per digit.
space_complexity_explanation: The operation modifies the array in place; a new array is only needed if all digits are 9.
key_insight: Only 9s cause carries — stop as soon as a non-9 digit is found. The only case needing an extra digit is all 9s (like 999 → 1000).

title: Pow(x, n)
hints: Naive multiplication is O(n). Can you do better using repeated squaring?
x^n = (x^(n/2))^2 for even n, and x * (x^(n/2))^2 for odd n.
Handle negative exponents by computing 1/x^|n|.
approach: Binary exponentiation (fast power). If n is even, x^n = (x^(n/2))^2. If n is odd, x^n = x * x^(n-1). Handle negative n by inverting x and negating n.
time_complexity_explanation: Each recursive call squares the problem size, reducing n by half each time, giving O(log n) multiplications.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth is O(log n), one frame per halving of the exponent.
key_insight: Binary exponentiation squares the base and halves the exponent each step — reducing O(n) multiplications to O(log n).

title: Multiply Strings
hints: Simulate grade-school multiplication digit by digit.
The product of digit at position i and j contributes to position i+j and i+j+1 in the result.
Accumulate products at each position and handle carries at the end.
approach: Simulate manual multiplication. Create a result array of size m+n. For each pair of digits (i, j), add their product to result[i+j+1] and carry to result[i+j]. Convert the result array to a string, trimming leading zeros.
solution_python:
def multiply(self, num1: str, num2: str) -> str:
    if num1 == '0' or num2 == '0':
        return '0'
    m, n = len(num1), len(num2)
    res = [0] * (m + n)
    for i in range(m - 1, -1, -1):
        for j in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            mul = int(num1[i]) * int(num2[j])
            p1, p2 = i + j, i + j + 1
            total = mul + res[p2]
            res[p2] = total % 10
            res[p1] += total // 10
    result = ''.join(map(str, res))
    return result.lstrip('0') or '0'
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m digits in num1 is multiplied with each of the n digits in num2, giving O(m × n) single-digit multiplications.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array has at most m + n digits to store intermediate sums and the final product.
key_insight: Digit i * digit j always contributes to positions i+j and i+j+1 in the result — this position mapping makes grade-school multiplication systematic.

title: Detect Squares
hints: For a query point, try all points that could be a diagonal corner of a square.
A diagonal corner has both different x and y, with |dx| == |dy| (forming a square).
Check if the other two corners exist using a hash map of point counts.
approach: Store point counts in a hash map. For a query point (qx, qy), iterate through all stored points (px, py) where |px-qx| == |py-qy| != 0 (diagonal). Check if the other two corners (qx,py) and (px,qy) exist. Multiply counts.
time_complexity_explanation: For each count query, we iterate through all stored points to find diagonal matches, checking two corners per candidate.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores the count of all n points that have been added.
key_insight: For each stored point as a potential diagonal corner, the other two corners are uniquely determined — just check if they exist in the point map.

title: Reconstruct Itinerary
hints: This is an Eulerian path problem — visit every edge exactly once.
Use Hierholzer's algorithm: DFS and add nodes to the result in post-order.
Sort destinations lexicographically so the smallest itinerary is found first.
approach: Build an adjacency list with sorted destinations. Use Hierholzer's algorithm (DFS): visit destinations greedily in lexicographic order, and add the current node to the result after all edges from it are used. Reverse the result.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the adjacency lists takes O(E log E). The DFS visits each edge exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list and the result path together store O(E) entries.
key_insight: Hierholzer's algorithm finds an Eulerian path by post-order DFS — nodes are added to result only after all their outgoing edges are consumed.

title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
hints: This is a minimum spanning tree problem with Manhattan distance as edge weights.
Use Prim's algorithm with a min-heap to greedily add the cheapest edge.
Start from any point, always connect the nearest unvisited point.
approach: Prim's algorithm. Start from point 0. Use a min-heap of (cost, point). Greedily add the cheapest edge connecting to an unvisited point. Repeat until all points are connected.
time_complexity_explanation: Prim's algorithm processes all O(n²) possible edges, each with a heap operation costing O(log(n²)) = O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list or distance array stores O(n²) edges (complete graph of n points).
key_insight: Prim's algorithm greedily grows the MST by always adding the cheapest edge to an unvisited node — the min-heap efficiently finds this edge.

title: Network Delay Time
hints: Find the shortest path from source to all nodes — the answer is the maximum of these.
Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap for weighted shortest paths.
If any node is unreachable, return -1.
approach: Dijkstra's algorithm from node k. Use a min-heap to process nodes in order of shortest distance. Track the time to reach each node. The answer is the maximum time to reach any node, or -1 if not all are reachable.
time_complexity_explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm processes each vertex once and each edge once, with heap operations costing O(log V) each.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list stores all edges, and the distance array and heap store up to V entries.
key_insight: Dijkstra's finds shortest paths from source to all nodes — the network delay is the maximum shortest path (the last node to receive the signal).

title: Swim in Rising Water
hints: You need to find the minimum time to travel from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1).
The time to traverse a path is the maximum elevation along that path.
Use Dijkstra's with max-elevation as the 'cost' to minimize the path's bottleneck.
approach: Modified Dijkstra's where the 'distance' is the maximum elevation along the path. Use a min-heap keyed by the max elevation to reach each cell. The first time we reach (n-1,n-1), we have the minimum possible maximum elevation.
time_complexity_explanation: Modified Dijkstra's processes each cell, with up to n² heap operations each costing O(log(n²)).
space_complexity_explanation: The visited matrix and the min-heap each store up to n² entries.
key_insight: This is a minimax path problem — Dijkstra's minimizes the maximum elevation, treating max(path_cost, neighbor_elevation) as the edge weight.

title: Alien Dictionary
hints: Compare adjacent words to extract character ordering constraints.
The first differing character between two adjacent words gives an edge: char1 < char2.
Use topological sort on the character graph; detect cycles (invalid ordering).
approach: Build a directed graph of character orderings by comparing adjacent words. Extract edges from the first differing character. Topological sort the graph using DFS with cycle detection. Return the sorted order.
time_complexity: O(C) where C is total characters across all words
time_complexity_explanation: We compare each pair of adjacent words (summing to C total characters) to extract edges, then topological sort is O(V + E) where V ≤ 26.
space_complexity_explanation: The graph has at most 26 nodes and at most 26² edges, which is constant regardless of input size.
key_insight: Adjacent word comparisons yield character ordering edges. Topological sort of the character graph produces the alien alphabet — cycles mean invalid input.

title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
hints: This is shortest path with a constraint on the number of edges (stops).
Bellman-Ford with k+1 iterations naturally limits the path to at most k stops.
Each iteration relaxes edges using only the previous iteration's distances.
approach: Bellman-Ford for k+1 iterations. In each iteration, for each flight (edge), try to relax the destination's cost using the source's cost from the PREVIOUS iteration. This limits paths to at most k+1 edges (k stops).
time_complexity_explanation: Bellman-Ford runs k+1 relaxation rounds, each processing all E flight edges once.
space_complexity_explanation: Two distance arrays of size n (current and previous round) are used for the relaxation.
key_insight: Bellman-Ford with k+1 iterations finds shortest paths using at most k+1 edges — using previous iteration's values prevents exceeding the stop limit.

title: Longest Common Prefix
hints: Compare characters across all strings at the same position.
Use the first string as the reference prefix and shorten it whenever a mismatch is found.
For each string, keep trimming the prefix from the end until it matches the start of that string.
approach: Use the first string as the initial prefix candidate. For each subsequent string, repeatedly trim the last character of the prefix until the current string starts with it. If the prefix becomes empty, return an empty string.
time_complexity_explanation: S is the total number of characters across all strings. In the worst case we compare every character once.
space_complexity_explanation: We only store the prefix in-place, reusing the first string's memory.
key_insight: The common prefix can only shrink as you examine more strings — use the first string as a baseline and trim it down.

title: Roman to Integer
hints: Map each Roman symbol to its integer value and scan left to right.
Roman numerals subtract when a smaller value appears before a larger value.
If the current symbol's value is less than the next symbol's value, subtract it; otherwise add it.
approach: Create a map of Roman symbols to values. Iterate through the string; if the current character's value is less than the next character's value, subtract it from the result; otherwise add it. This handles all subtraction cases (IV, IX, XL, etc.) naturally.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the string once, and each lookup in the map is O(1). n is the length of the string.
space_complexity_explanation: The Roman numeral map has a fixed size of 7 entries regardless of input.
key_insight: A smaller value appearing before a larger value always means subtraction — this single rule handles all six special cases (IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM).

title: Integer to Roman
hints: Think greedily — always use the largest Roman numeral that fits into the remaining value.
Pre-define all 13 value-symbol pairs including subtraction forms like 900 (CM) and 400 (CD).
Repeatedly subtract the largest value that fits and append the corresponding symbol.
approach: Pre-define all 13 value-symbol pairs in descending order (including subtractive forms like 900=CM, 400=CD). Greedily pick the largest value that fits into num, append its symbol, subtract the value, and repeat until num reaches zero.
time_complexity_explanation: The input is bounded by 3999, so the number of iterations is bounded by a constant (at most 15 'M' symbols for 3000).
space_complexity_explanation: The lookup tables and output string are bounded by constant size given the fixed input range.
key_insight: By including all six subtractive forms (IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM) in the value table, the greedy algorithm works perfectly without any special-case logic.

title: Next Permutation
hints: The next permutation is found by making the smallest possible increase from the right side.
Find the rightmost position where the sequence stops decreasing (a 'dip'), then find the smallest element to its right that is larger, and swap them.
After swapping, reverse the suffix to its smallest (ascending) order.
approach: Scan from right to find the first index i where nums[i] < nums[i+1] (the descent point). Then find the smallest element greater than nums[i] in the suffix and swap them. Finally, reverse the suffix starting at i+1 to make it the smallest possible arrangement.
time_complexity_explanation: We make at most two linear scans (finding i, finding j) and one linear reversal — all O(n) where n is the array length.
space_complexity_explanation: All operations are performed in-place with only a constant number of pointer variables.
key_insight: The suffix to the right of the descent point is always in descending order, so reversing it gives the smallest possible arrangement for that suffix.

title: Jump Game II
hints: Think in 'levels' like BFS — all positions reachable with the same number of jumps form one level.
Track the farthest reachable index within the current level; when you reach the end of the level, you must jump.
You only need to jump when you've exhausted all positions in the current 'frontier'.
approach: Use a greedy BFS-like approach. Track currentEnd (end of current jump level) and farthest (maximum reach found so far). Iterate through indices; whenever i reaches currentEnd, increment jumps and set currentEnd to farthest. Stop before the last index since reaching it doesn't require another jump.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array where n is the length of nums.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three integer variables are maintained regardless of input size.
key_insight: Model jumps as BFS levels — always extend to the farthest reachable position within each level, and count one jump per level transition.

title: Gas Station
hints: If the total gas is at least the total cost, a valid starting station must exist.
Simulate driving around and track your running fuel. When it goes negative, the current start is invalid.
Whenever your tank drops below zero, reset the start to the next station — that's the only viable candidate.
approach: First check if total gas >= total cost; if not, return -1. Then greedily find the start: maintain a running tank total. Whenever the tank goes negative, the current starting station can't work, so reset the start to the next station and reset the tank. The final candidate is guaranteed to be valid.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes — one for summing totals and one for finding the start — each O(n) where n is the number of stations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: If you can't reach station j from station i, then no station between i and j can be a valid starting point either — this lets you skip ahead rather than brute-force every start.

title: Sqrt(x)
hints: The integer square root is the largest integer whose square does not exceed x.
Binary search between 1 and x/2 for the answer.
When mid*mid <= x < (mid+1)*(mid+1), mid is the answer — return hi after the loop ends.
approach: Binary search in the range [1, x/2]. For each midpoint, compare mid*mid to x. If equal, return mid immediately. If mid*mid < x, search higher; otherwise search lower. When the loop ends, hi holds the floor of the square root.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration, giving O(log x) iterations over a range of size x/2.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer variables are used.
key_insight: After the binary search loop, hi always points to the largest integer whose square is <= x — the floor square root.

title: Perfect Squares
hints: Think of this as an unbounded coin change problem where the coins are perfect squares.
Build dp[i] = minimum number of perfect squares that sum to i.
For each i, try subtracting every perfect square j*j <= i and take the minimum.
approach: Use bottom-up DP: dp[0] = 0 and dp[i] = min(dp[i - j*j] + 1) for all j where j*j <= i. This is equivalent to the coin change problem where the coins are 1, 4, 9, 16, ... Build the table from 1 to n.
time_complexity_explanation: For each value from 1 to n, we try all perfect squares up to it, which is O(sqrt(i)) per value, giving O(n * sqrt(n)) total.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array has n+1 entries.
key_insight: This is identical in structure to the coin change problem — perfect squares are the 'coins' and you want the minimum count to sum to n.

title: Ugly Number II
hints: Generate ugly numbers in order — each ugly number is 2x, 3x, or 5x of a previous ugly number.
Use three pointers tracking which ugly number each factor (2, 3, 5) should multiply next.
Always pick the minimum next candidate and advance the pointer(s) that produced it.
approach: Maintain an array of ugly numbers and three pointers p2, p3, p5. At each step, the next ugly number is the minimum of ugly[p2]*2, ugly[p3]*3, and ugly[p5]*5. Advance all pointers that produced the minimum to avoid duplicates.
time_complexity_explanation: We compute exactly n ugly numbers, one per iteration, with O(1) work per step.
space_complexity_explanation: The ugly array stores all n generated ugly numbers.
key_insight: All ugly numbers are products of previous ugly numbers times 2, 3, or 5 — three pointers ensure we never miss or duplicate a candidate.

title: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
hints: There are two cases: the max subarray is either entirely within the array, or it wraps around.
A wrapping subarray equals the total sum minus the minimum non-wrapping subarray.
Run Kadane's algorithm to find both the maximum subarray and the minimum subarray sums.
approach: Run Kadane's algorithm simultaneously for both maximum and minimum subarray sums. The answer is the maximum of: (1) the standard max subarray sum, or (2) total - min subarray sum (the wrapping case). If all elements are negative, the wrapping case would give 0 which is wrong — in that case return the max subarray sum.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def maxSubarraySumCircular(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
        total = sum(nums)
        max_sum = nums[0]
        cur_max = 0
        min_sum = nums[0]
        cur_min = 0
        for num in nums:
            cur_max = max(cur_max + num, num)
            max_sum = max(max_sum, cur_max)
            cur_min = min(cur_min + num, num)
            min_sum = min(min_sum, cur_min)
        return max(max_sum, total - min_sum) if max_sum > 0 else max_sum
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array computing both max and min Kadane values simultaneously.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of tracking variables are needed.
key_insight: A circular subarray that wraps around is equivalent to the total sum minus some non-wrapping middle subarray — so minimizing the middle maximizes the wrap-around portion.

title: Longest Palindromic Subsequence
hints: Think about matching characters from both ends of the string.
Define dp[i][j] as the length of the longest palindromic subsequence in s[i..j].
If s[i] == s[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 2; otherwise dp[i][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
approach: Use interval DP where dp[i][j] = longest palindromic subsequence in s[i..j]. Base case: dp[i][i] = 1. For each length from 2 to n, if s[i] == s[j], extend the inner result by 2; otherwise take the max of skipping either endpoint.
time_complexity_explanation: We fill an n×n DP table with O(1) work per cell, giving O(n^2) total where n is the string length.
space_complexity_explanation: The DP table is n×n.
key_insight: When both endpoints match, we can include them and recurse on the inner substring — this is the key recurrence that makes interval DP work here.

title: Palindrome Partitioning II
hints: Precompute a 2D palindrome table so you can check any substring in O(1).
Define cuts[j] = minimum number of cuts for s[0..j-1].
For each j, try every start i: if s[i..j-1] is a palindrome, cuts[j] = min(cuts[j], cuts[i] + 1).
approach: First build a 2D boolean table is_pal[i][j] using DP (s[i]==s[j] and inner is palindrome). Then compute cuts[j] = minimum cuts for the first j characters by trying all palindromic endings. The answer is cuts[n] where -1 means the whole string is already a palindrome.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the palindrome table takes O(n^2). The cuts DP is also O(n^2). n is the string length.
space_complexity_explanation: The palindrome table requires O(n^2) space.
key_insight: Precomputing palindrome substrings in O(n^2) transforms the cut DP into a clean linear recurrence lookup.

title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
hints: For a perfect binary tree, every node has exactly two children — use this property.
Once you set the next pointers at level k, you can use them to traverse level k and set level k+1 pointers.
For each node with a next pointer set: node.left.next = node.right, and node.right.next = node.next.left (if node.next exists).
approach: Start at the leftmost node of each level. For each node at the current level, connect its left child's next to its right child, and its right child's next to the left child of its next sibling. Move to the next level by following the leftmost path.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit every node exactly once to set its children's next pointers. n is the number of nodes.
space_complexity_explanation: We only use a constant number of pointer variables — no queue or recursion stack needed.
key_insight: Once the next pointers at level k are set, you can traverse level k using those pointers to wire up level k+1 — making this an O(1) space solution.

title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
hints: The flattened list follows pre-order traversal (root, left, right).
Process nodes in reverse pre-order: right subtree first, then left, then root.
Keep a 'prev' pointer; set node.right = prev and node.left = None at each step.
approach: Process nodes in reverse pre-order (right, left, root) with a global prev pointer. At each node, set node.right = prev, node.left = None, then update prev = node. This builds the linked list from right to left in one pass without extra space.
time_complexity_explanation: Every node is visited exactly once in the DFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack uses O(h) space where h is the height of the tree (O(log n) for balanced, O(n) for skewed).
key_insight: By traversing in reverse pre-order (right, left, root), each node can be wired to the already-processed 'prev' node, building the list in correct pre-order without needing extra memory.

title: Count Complete Tree Nodes
hints: A naive DFS is O(n) — can you do better by exploiting the complete tree property?
Compare the height of the leftmost path versus the rightmost path.
If the heights are equal, the left subtree is a perfect binary tree — its count is 2^h - 1. Recurse on the right subtree.
approach: At each node, compute left height (following left pointers) and right height (following right pointers). If equal, the subtree is a perfect binary tree with 2^h - 1 nodes. Otherwise, recurse on both children. This achieves O(log^2 n) by reducing tree height each recursion level.
time_complexity_explanation: At each level we do O(log n) work to compute heights, and we recurse O(log n) levels deep, giving O(log^2 n).
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack is O(log n) deep since we always eliminate at least half the tree.
key_insight: In a complete binary tree, comparing the leftmost and rightmost heights tells you whether the subtree is perfect (and thus countable in O(1)) — this is the key to beating O(n).

title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
hints: Standard level-order BFS collects nodes in left-to-right order per level.
For odd levels (0-indexed), keep left-to-right; for even levels, reverse the collected level.
A toggle flag alternating each level is sufficient — no need to change the BFS order itself.
approach: Perform standard BFS with a queue. After collecting all nodes at each level, check the direction flag: append the level as-is for left-to-right, or reversed for right-to-left. Toggle the direction flag after each level.
time_complexity_explanation: Every node is visited exactly once during BFS.
space_complexity_explanation: The queue holds at most O(n/2) nodes at the widest level, and the result stores all n values.
key_insight: BFS naturally gives you left-to-right order — simply reversing alternate levels gives the zigzag effect without any change to the traversal.

title: All Paths From Source to Target
hints: Use DFS from node 0 and collect paths that reach the last node.
Since the graph is a DAG, no cycle detection is needed.
Backtrack by appending a neighbor, recursing, then removing it from the path.
approach: Run DFS from node 0, maintaining the current path. When the current node equals n-1 (the target), add a copy of the path to results. For each neighbor, push it to the path, recurse, then pop it (backtrack). The DAG structure guarantees termination.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case there are 2^n paths, each of length O(n). n is the number of nodes.
space_complexity_explanation: Storing all paths requires O(2^n * n) space in the worst case; the recursion stack is O(n).
key_insight: Because the graph is a DAG (directed, acyclic), simple DFS with backtracking finds all paths without needing a visited set.

title: Find the Celebrity
hints: A celebrity knows nobody but is known by everybody — use this asymmetry to eliminate candidates.
If person A knows person B, A is not the celebrity. If A does not know B, B is not the celebrity.
One pass to find a candidate, then verify the candidate with a second pass.
approach: Start with candidate 0. For each subsequent person i, if the candidate knows i, update candidate to i (the old candidate is eliminated). After one pass, verify the surviving candidate: check that they know nobody and that everyone knows them using two more linear scans.
time_complexity_explanation: The elimination pass makes n-1 calls to knows(), and the verification pass makes at most 2*(n-1) calls — O(n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single candidate variable is maintained.
key_insight: Every call to knows() eliminates exactly one person from candidacy — either the current candidate (if they know i) or i (if the candidate doesn't know them) — making O(n) elimination possible.

title: LFU Cache
hints: You need O(1) get and put — a heap would give O(log n) for updates, so use hash maps instead.
Maintain three structures: key→value, key→frequency, and frequency→ordered set of keys.
Track the minimum frequency so eviction is O(1): remove the LRU key from the min-frequency bucket.
approach: Use three hash maps: key_val (key→value), key_freq (key→frequency), and freq_keys (frequency→OrderedDict of keys in insertion order). Track min_freq. On get/put, update frequency counts, move the key to the correct frequency bucket, and update min_freq accordingly. Evict the LRU item from the min_freq bucket when capacity is exceeded.
time_complexity_explanation: All operations — get, put, and the internal _update — perform only O(1) hash map and ordered dictionary operations.
space_complexity_explanation: We store at most 'capacity' keys across the three hash map structures.
key_insight: Using an OrderedDict (insertion-ordered map) per frequency bucket gives O(1) LRU eviction within a frequency group, and tracking min_freq explicitly avoids any O(log n) heap operations.

title: Sort Colors
hints: Count 0s, 1s, and 2s then overwrite — but can you do it in one pass?
Use three pointers: lo (boundary of 0s), mid (current), hi (boundary of 2s).
When nums[mid] is 0, swap with lo and advance both; when 2, swap with hi and only advance hi; when 1, just advance mid.
approach: Apply Dijkstra's Dutch National Flag algorithm with three pointers. lo and mid start at 0; hi starts at n-1. When nums[mid] is 0, swap with lo and advance both pointers. When 2, swap with hi and only decrease hi (don't advance mid since the swapped value is unknown). When 1, just advance mid.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass with three pointers — mid and hi together span the array exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three pointer variables are used; sorting is done in-place.
key_insight: When swapping a 2 from mid with hi, you don't advance mid because the newly placed value at mid (from hi) hasn't been examined yet — this is the subtle but crucial detail of the Dutch National Flag algorithm.

title: Rotate Array
hints: Think about what a k-step right rotation looks like: the last k elements move to the front.
One approach: reverse the entire array, then reverse the first k elements, then reverse the rest.
Don't forget to handle k > n by taking k % n.
approach: Use the three-reversal trick: (1) reverse the entire array, (2) reverse the first k elements, (3) reverse the remaining n-k elements. This runs in O(n) time and O(1) space. Handle k > n with k %= n.
time_complexity_explanation: Three reversal passes each touch at most n elements in total.
space_complexity_explanation: All operations are in-place with only index variables.
key_insight: Reversing the whole array and then reversing two halves is equivalent to a rotation — this triple-reversal trick achieves O(1) space vs. the intuitive O(n) extra array approach.

title: Spiral Matrix II
hints: Fill the matrix layer by layer, starting from the outermost ring moving inward.
Track four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right — shrink them after filling each edge.
Fill right along top, down along right, left along bottom, up along left, then tighten boundaries.
approach: Maintain four boundaries (top, bottom, left, right) and a counter num starting at 1. Fill the top row left-to-right, right column top-to-bottom, bottom row right-to-left, and left column bottom-to-top. After each direction, shrink the corresponding boundary. Repeat until all n^2 cells are filled.
time_complexity_explanation: Every cell in the n×n matrix is filled exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The output matrix is not counted as extra space; only four boundary variables are needed.
key_insight: Maintaining four shrinking boundaries elegantly handles all edge cases including the center cell of odd-sized matrices.

title: Power of Two
hints: Powers of two have exactly one bit set in their binary representation.
Subtracting 1 from a power of two flips the set bit and sets all lower bits.
n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit — for powers of two this yields 0.
approach: A number n is a power of two if and only if n > 0 and n & (n-1) == 0. The expression n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit; for powers of two (which have exactly one set bit), this always produces 0.
time_complexity_explanation: A single bitwise AND operation and comparison — constant time regardless of input.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space is used.
key_insight: Powers of two have exactly one set bit, so n & (n-1) always removes that lone bit, giving 0 — an elegant O(1) bitwise check.

title: Longest Turbulent Subarray
hints: A turbulent subarray alternates strictly between increasing and decreasing comparisons.
Use a sliding window: expand right and shrink left when the turbulence condition breaks.
The window breaks when two consecutive comparisons have the same sign or a comparison is equal.
approach: Use a sliding window with a left pointer. For each right >= 1, compute c = sign(arr[right] - arr[right-1]). If right >= 2, compare with the previous comparison sign: if c == 0 reset left to right; if c equals the previous sign, the last valid window starts at right-1. Update result each iteration.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) work per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer and comparison variables are used.
key_insight: When the turbulence breaks due to two consecutive same-direction comparisons, the new window must start from the previous element (right-1), not right — because (right-1, right) still forms a valid 2-element turbulent window.

title: Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards
hints: You take k cards from either end — equivalently, you leave n-k consecutive cards in the middle.
Instead of maximizing the cards you take, minimize the sum of the n-k cards you leave.
Slide a window of size n-k across the array to find the minimum subarray sum.
approach: Reframe: picking k cards from either end leaves a contiguous subarray of size n-k in the middle. Use a sliding window of fixed size n-k to find the minimum subarray sum. The answer is the total sum minus this minimum window sum.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass to compute total, one pass for the sliding window — both O(n) where n is the number of cards.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of sum variables are maintained.
key_insight: Picking k cards from either end is equivalent to NOT picking a contiguous window of n-k cards — minimizing the middle window maximizes the score.

title: Subarray Sum Equals K
hints: Think about prefix sums: if prefix[j] - prefix[i] == k, then subarray i..j sums to k.
Store prefix sums in a hash map as you iterate. At each index, check if (current_prefix - k) exists in the map.
Initialize the map with {0: 1} to handle subarrays that start from index 0.
approach: Compute prefix sums on the fly. For each index j, if prefix[j] - k equals some earlier prefix[i], then the subarray from i+1 to j sums to k. Use a hash map to store the count of each prefix sum seen so far, allowing O(1) lookup.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once. Each hash map lookup and insert is O(1) on average.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most n+1 distinct prefix sums in the worst case.
key_insight: prefix[j] - prefix[i] == k means subarray (i, j] sums to k. Store prefix sum frequencies to count all valid (i, j) pairs in one pass.

title: Majority Element
hints: A simple O(n) space solution: use a hash map to count occurrences of each element.
For O(1) space, think about what happens when two different elements cancel each other out.
Boyer-Moore Voting: maintain a candidate and a count. Increment count if current element matches candidate, otherwise decrement. Reset candidate when count hits 0.
approach: Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm: since the majority element appears more than n/2 times, it will always 'survive' the cancellation process. Maintain a candidate and a count. When count drops to 0, the current element becomes the new candidate. The majority element is guaranteed to be the final candidate.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables (candidate and count) are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The majority element has more occurrences than all other elements combined, so it can never be fully cancelled out by non-majority elements.

title: Move Zeroes
hints: Use a slow pointer to track the position where the next non-zero element should go.
Iterate with a fast pointer. Whenever you find a non-zero element, place it at the slow pointer and advance the slow pointer.
After placing all non-zero elements, fill the rest of the array with zeroes.
approach: Two-pointer approach: keep a 'write' pointer that tracks where the next non-zero value should be placed. Iterate through the array with a 'read' pointer; whenever a non-zero is found, write it at the write pointer index and advance the write pointer. Finally, fill positions from the write pointer to the end with zeros.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass with the read pointer plus a final fill pass — both O(n). Total is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: In-place modification using only two index variables.
key_insight: Separate the problem into two steps: compact non-zero elements to the front, then zero-fill the tail — both achievable in a single conceptual pass.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
hints: Think recursively: for each node, check whether p or q lives in its left subtree, right subtree, or is the node itself.
If p is found in one subtree and q in the other, the current node is the LCA.
If both are in the same subtree, recurse deeper into that subtree.
approach: Recursive DFS: for each node, recursively search the left and right subtrees. If a node equals p or q, return it. If both left and right recursive calls return non-null results, the current node is the LCA (p and q are split across subtrees). Otherwise, return whichever side returned a non-null result.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit every node at most once in the worst case (when LCA is the root).
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth equals the height h of the tree. O(n) in the worst case for a skewed tree, O(log n) for a balanced tree.
key_insight: When the recursive search finds p in one subtree and q in the other, the current node must be their lowest common ancestor.

title: Decode String
hints: Use a stack to handle nested brackets. Push the current string and count when you see '[', pop and repeat when you see ']'.
Build the current number digit-by-digit before pushing it. Build the current string character-by-character.
When you hit ']', pop the previous string and repeat count, then concatenate with the previous string.
approach: Use a stack that stores pairs of (current_string, repeat_count). When seeing a digit, build the number. When seeing '[', push (current_string, current_k) and reset both. When seeing ']', pop the last (prev_str, k) and set current_string = prev_str + current_string * k. When seeing a letter, append to current_string.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is processed once, but string repetitions can expand the result. The total output length (and thus work) can be O(n * max_k) where max_k is the largest repeat count.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds at most O(n) elements in the worst case (deeply nested brackets).
key_insight: A stack naturally models nested bracket structure — push context on '[', pop and repeat on ']'.

title: Merge Strings Alternately
hints: Use a single index to step through both strings simultaneously.
At each step, append the character from word1 if available, then the character from word2 if available.
Once one string is exhausted, simply append the remainder of the other.
approach: Use a single index i. While i is within either string's bounds, append word1[i] (if in range) then word2[i] (if in range). This naturally interleaves characters and handles strings of different lengths.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate once through both strings with a single index, touching each character exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The result string has length m + n where m and n are the lengths of the two input strings.
key_insight: A single index stepping through both strings simultaneously handles different lengths gracefully without needing separate pointers.

title: Next Greater Element I
hints: A brute-force approach compares each element in nums1 against elements to its right in nums2 — O(m*n).
For a faster approach, pre-compute the next greater element for every number in nums2 using a monotonic stack.
Maintain a decreasing stack. When you find a larger element, it's the next greater for everything popped from the stack.
approach: Pre-process nums2 with a monotonic decreasing stack to build a map from each number to its next greater element. Iterate nums2: for each element, pop stack elements that are smaller (the current element is their next greater), then push the current element. Finally, look up each nums1 element in the map.
time_complexity_explanation: We process nums2 once with the stack in O(n), then query each of the m elements in nums1 in O(1) via the hash map.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and hash map together store at most n entries (one per element in nums2).
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing stack efficiently finds the next greater element for all elements in a single pass — each element is pushed and popped at most once.

title: Single Element in a Sorted Array
hints: In a sorted array where all pairs appear together, pairs before the single element appear at (even, odd) indices; pairs after appear at (odd, even) indices.
Use binary search on even indices. If nums[mid] == nums[mid+1], the single element is to the right; otherwise it's at mid or to the left.
Always check mid at an even index to reason about pair alignment cleanly.
approach: Binary search on even indices only. Before the single element, each pair starts at an even index (nums[even] == nums[even+1]). After it, pairs start at odd indices (nums[odd] == nums[odd-1]). Midpoint mid should always be even (round down if odd). If nums[mid] == nums[mid+1], the single element is to the right of mid+1; otherwise, it's at mid or to the left.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space on each step, giving O(log n) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointers (lo, hi) are used — constant space.
key_insight: The pair-index parity flips at the position of the single element: pairs before it start at even indices, pairs after start at odd indices.

title: Diagonal Traverse
hints: An m×n matrix has m+n-1 diagonals. Elements on the same diagonal share the same sum of their indices (row + col).
For diagonal d (where d = row + col), traverse elements where row+col == d. Alternate the direction on each diagonal.
Collect each diagonal into a list, reverse alternate diagonals, then flatten.
approach: Group elements by their diagonal index d = row + col. For each diagonal, collect its elements in order. Reverse alternate diagonals (even-indexed diagonals go up-right, odd go down-left). Flatten the results into the output array.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit every cell exactly once to group them, then produce the output array — both O(m*n).
space_complexity_explanation: The diagonal arrays together hold all m*n elements, plus the output array of the same size.
key_insight: Elements on the same anti-diagonal share the same row+col sum. Alternating reversal of diagonals produces the zigzag traversal pattern.

title: Palindrome Linked List
hints: Find the middle of the linked list using fast/slow pointers.
