Reverse the second half of the list in-place.
Compare the first half and the reversed second half node by node.
approach: 1) Find the middle using slow/fast pointers. 2) Reverse the second half of the list. 3) Compare the first half with the reversed second half — if all values match, it's a palindrome. Optionally restore the list afterward.
time_complexity_explanation: Finding the middle, reversing the second half, and comparing each take O(n) time.
space_complexity_explanation: In-place reversal uses only a constant number of pointers.
key_insight: Reversing the second half in-place enables O(1)-space palindrome checking without copying values to an array.

title: Middle of the Linked List
hints: A naive approach counts the list length, then traverses again to n/2 — O(n) but two passes.
Use two pointers: a slow pointer moving one step at a time and a fast pointer moving two steps.
When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer is at the middle.
approach: Fast/slow pointer technique: slow moves one node at a time, fast moves two nodes. When fast reaches the end (null or last node), slow is pointing at the middle. For even-length lists, this returns the second middle node (per the problem requirement).
time_complexity_explanation: Both pointers traverse the list at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are used, independent of list size.
key_insight: Fast pointer moves at 2x speed, so when it finishes the list, the slow pointer has covered exactly half the distance.

title: Fibonacci Number
hints: The recursive definition is F(0)=0, F(1)=1, F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2). Naive recursion is O(2^n).
Use memoization (top-down DP) to cache sub-results, reducing to O(n) time.
An iterative bottom-up approach with just two variables achieves O(n) time and O(1) space.
approach: Iterative bottom-up: start with a=0 (F(0)) and b=1 (F(1)). In each step, compute a+b, then slide the window: a becomes b and b becomes the new sum. After n iterations, a holds F(n).
time_complexity_explanation: The loop runs n-1 times, each doing constant work.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables are maintained regardless of n.
key_insight: You only ever need the two most recent Fibonacci numbers to compute the next one, so the entire DP table reduces to two variables.

title: Max Consecutive Ones
hints: Track the current streak of consecutive ones as you scan.
Reset the current count to 0 whenever you hit a 0.
Update the global maximum at each step.
approach: Single-pass scan: maintain current_count and max_count. Increment current_count on 1, reset to 0 on 0. Update max_count at each step.
time_complexity_explanation: A single linear scan through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two counter variables are used.
key_insight: Reset the current streak to 0 on any 0 encountered; track the running maximum.

title: Find K Closest Elements
hints: The answer is a contiguous window of k elements in the sorted array. Binary search for the optimal left boundary of that window.
Compare the distance of the left boundary element vs. the element k positions to its right. Choose the side that keeps the closer elements.
Binary search on the left pointer: if x - arr[mid] > arr[mid + k] - x, move left boundary right.
approach: Binary search for the left boundary of the result window. The window always has k elements. Compare arr[mid] (left candidate) and arr[mid+k] (right candidate) against x. If arr[mid+k] is closer, shift the window right (lo = mid + 1). Otherwise keep or shift left (hi = mid). The final window [lo, lo+k) is the answer.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs in O(log(n-k)) to find the window start, then O(k) to extract the result slice.
space_complexity_explanation: The output array of k elements is the only non-constant space used.
key_insight: Binary search on the left boundary of a fixed-size window — compare the two endpoints' distance to x to decide which direction to move.

title: Intersection of Two Arrays
hints: Convert one array to a set for O(1) lookup.
Iterate the other array and collect elements that exist in the set.
Use a second set to avoid duplicates in the result.
approach: Convert nums1 to a set. Iterate nums2; if an element is in the set, add it to a result set. Return the result set as a list. Using sets on both sides ensures uniqueness without sorting.
time_complexity_explanation: Building set1 is O(m). Filtering nums2 is O(n). Both operations are linear.
space_complexity_explanation: The set of nums1 takes O(m) space and the result set takes at most O(min(m,n)) space.
key_insight: Converting one array to a set reduces the membership check from O(n) to O(1), turning the naive O(m*n) approach into O(m+n).

title: Asteroid Collision
hints: A collision only happens when a positive asteroid is followed by a negative asteroid (they move toward each other).
Use a stack. Push positive asteroids. When a negative asteroid arrives, compare with the top of the stack.
The negative asteroid destroys smaller positives, is destroyed by a larger positive, and both are destroyed if equal.
approach: Iterate through asteroids. Push positive asteroids onto the stack. For a negative asteroid, compare its magnitude with the top of the stack: if the top is smaller, pop it (destroyed) and continue; if equal, pop the top and skip the current; if the top is larger, skip the current. If the stack is empty or the top is negative, push the current asteroid.
time_complexity_explanation: Each asteroid is pushed and popped at most once, giving amortized O(1) per asteroid.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds at most n asteroids in the worst case (no collisions).
key_insight: Only right-moving (positive) followed by left-moving (negative) asteroids collide. A stack naturally represents the active right-moving asteroids waiting to collide.

title: Next Greater Element II
hints: The array is circular, so elements can wrap around. Simulate this by iterating 2n times using modulo indexing.
Use a monotonic decreasing stack of indices. For each element, pop indices whose values are smaller than the current element — they've found their next greater.
On the second pass (indices n to 2n-1), don't push to the stack — just resolve remaining unresolved elements.
approach: Iterate indices 0 to 2n-1 using index % n. Use a monotonic decreasing stack (stores indices). For each position, pop stack entries whose values are less than the current value and record the answer. Push current index (only if i < n to avoid duplicate pushes on the second pass). Initialize result array with -1.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate 2n times. Each element is pushed and popped from the stack at most once, so total stack operations are O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds at most n indices; the result array is n.
key_insight: Simulating circularity by iterating twice (using i % n) lets the standard monotonic stack technique handle the wrap-around case.

title: Search a 2D Matrix II
hints: Binary search each row independently — O(m log n) — but we can do better.
Start at the top-right corner. If the target is smaller, move left; if larger, move down.
This eliminates one row or column per step, giving O(m + n) total steps.
approach: Staircase search: start at the top-right corner (row=0, col=n-1). At each step: if matrix[row][col] equals target, return true. If it's greater than target, move left (col--). If less, move down (row++). Continue until row or col goes out of bounds.
time_complexity_explanation: Each step eliminates one row or one column. Starting with m rows and n columns, we take at most m+n steps.
space_complexity_explanation: Only row and col pointer variables are used.
key_insight: The top-right corner is uniquely positioned: it's the maximum of its row (allowing left elimination) and the minimum of its column (allowing downward search). This gives a deterministic elimination strategy.

title: Split Array Largest Sum
hints: Binary search on the answer: the minimum possible largest sum lies between max(nums) and sum(nums).
For a given mid value, greedily check if the array can be split into at most k subarrays each with sum ≤ mid.
If the greedy check passes (feasible), search lower; otherwise search higher.
approach: Binary search on the answer space [max(nums), sum(nums)]. For a candidate maximum sum 'mid', use a greedy check: iterate through nums, accumulating a running sum. If adding the next element would exceed mid, start a new subarray. Count the number of subarrays needed. If the count exceeds k, mid is too small; otherwise it may be achievable.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs O(log(sum - max)) iterations. Each iteration does an O(n) greedy feasibility check.
space_complexity_explanation: The feasibility check uses only a constant number of variables.
key_insight: Binary search on the answer (not the index) transforms an NP-hard-looking optimization problem into a feasibility check that's solvable greedily.

title: Implement Stack using Queues
hints: A stack is LIFO; a queue is FIFO. To simulate a stack pop (get the last-pushed element), you need to rearrange the queue.
One-queue approach: after each push, rotate the queue so the new element is at the front.
Rotate by dequeuing all n-1 elements and re-enqueuing them — the newly pushed element stays at the front.
approach: Single-queue approach: on push(x), enqueue x, then rotate the queue by dequeuing and re-enqueuing all elements that were there before x. This brings x to the front. Pop and top then simply dequeue from the front.
time_complexity_explanation: Each push rotates all n-1 existing elements, taking O(n). Pop and top are O(1) since the top-of-stack is always at the front of the queue.
space_complexity_explanation: The queue holds all n pushed elements.
key_insight: Rotating the queue after each push maintains the invariant that the most recently pushed element is always at the front — making pop and top O(1) at the cost of an O(n) push.
